Study Strategy

How Long Should You Study for the SPI Exam? A Realistic Timeline

6 min readApril 22, 2026

The most common question from SPI candidates: "how much time do I need?" The honest answer: most candidates need 4–8 weeks. The right timeline depends on your clinical background, available daily study hours, and whether you're using active recall or passive review.

The 30/60/90 Framework

30 Days

Strong clinical foundation + active recall study

You graduated recently from a CAHIIM-accredited DMS program, remember your physics coursework, and can study 45–60 minutes daily. With spaced-repetition flashcards and a full-length practice exam, 30 days is realistic.

60 Days

Moderate foundation or limited daily time

You're working clinically but haven't thought about physics since school, or can only study 20–30 minutes daily. 60 days gives enough repetitions to solidify weak domains without cramming.

90 Days

Returning to physics after a gap

You've been out of school for several years, or physics was never a strong subject. 90 days lets you build genuine understanding — and still leaves time for thorough review.

Why Study Method Matters More Than Hours

The research on learning is clear: active recall beats passive review every time. Reading notes is pleasant but ineffective. Flashcards that force retrieval — even when it's hard — produce far more durable learning. 20 minutes of spaced-repetition flashcards daily will typically outperform 2 hours of textbook reading.

Domain-Weighted Study Time

DomainWeight
Pulse-Echo Principles~25%
Doppler Physics~22%
Transducer Mechanisms~18%
Image Formation~17%
Artifact Recognition~11%
Bioeffects & Safety~7%

The Week Before

Use the final week for review, not new material. Take at least one full-length timed practice exam. Avoid cramming new content in the final 48 hours — interference hurts recall of what you already know. Light review and sleep serve you better.

Bottom line: 4–8 weeks, 30–45 minutes daily, active recall focus. SonoPrep's spaced-repetition engine shows you what you're missing — so every session moves the needle.
